heading: BIBLIOGRAFIA 2837
card_summary: This chunk is a section of the scholarly bibliography for Saverio Campanini's 2010 Bompiani edition of Zorzi's De Harmonia Mundi, covering entries from Lidzbarski through Perrone Compagni. It includes works on Renaissance Christian Kabbalah, Hermeticism, Jewish culture in Italy, and Francesco Zorzi (Giorgio Veneto) specifically, with multiple entries by Jean-François Maillard on Zorzi's harmonic theology, musical theory, and unpublished manuscripts. Other notable entries address Cusanus reception history, alchemical philosophy, the Venetian Inquisition, Ficino and alchemy, Agrippa's De Occulta Philosophia, Giles of Viterbo, Guillaume Postel, and the prisca theologia tradition. Several entries concern Zorzi's Dantesque poem and his integration of ancient pagan theology into Christian cosmology.
essay:
## Bibliography Section: BIBLIOGRAFIA 2837 (pp. 3001–3007)

### Nature and Scope

This chunk is a continuous alphabetical segment of the scholarly bibliography appended to Saverio Campanini's critical Italian edition of Francesco Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi* (Bompiani, Milan, 2010). The entries run from **L** (Lidzbarski) through **P** (Perrone Compagni), spanning roughly the mid-L through early-P alphabetical range. The bibliography is formatted in the standard European scholarly short-title citation style: an all-caps author-date key (e.g., `MAILLARD 1971`) followed by full bibliographic detail. This key system allows compact in-text citation throughout the 3,000-page edition.

### Organization

Entries are arranged alphabetically by author surname, then chronologically by date when an author has multiple entries. The bibliography is not subdivided by topic or primary/secondary distinction; it is a unified reference list for the entire critical apparatus, introductions, and notes of the edition. The range of languages is broad — Italian, French, German, English, Hebrew, and Latin works all appear — reflecting the multilingual character of Renaissance Kabbalistic scholarship and its modern reception.

### Scholarly Value

This section of the bibliography is of particular value for several research communities:

1. **Zorzi / Giorgio Veneto specialists**: Jean-François Maillard's cluster of entries (1971, 1972, 1972a, 1974, 1975, 1991, 1993, 1996, 1997) constitutes the most sustained body of modern scholarship on Zorzi prior to Campanini's own work, covering the genesis and structure of *De Harmonia Mundi*, its musical aspects, its Dantesque poem, its role in the Henry VIII divorce affair, and its unpublished manuscripts.

2. **Christian Kabbalah and Renaissance esotericism**: Entries such as Melczer 1984 (Hermeticism and Christian Kabbalah in Zorzi), Mopsik 1989 and 1996 (Hekhalot literature and Moshe de León), Muccillo 1988 and 1996 (prisca theologia, Hermeticism, Platonism), and Matton 1992a (Zorzi's integration of ancient gods into Christian theology) provide a dense network for situating Zorzi in the broader Renaissance esoteric tradition.

3. **Jewish-Christian intellectual exchange**: Entries on Shem Tov ibn Falaquera (Malter 1910–11), Hebrew manuscripts in the Bodleian (Neubauer 1886–1906) and British Museum (Margoliouth 1899–1915), Flavius Mithridates / Guglielmo Raimondo Moncada (Perani 2008), and Agostino Giustiniani's library (Mannucci 1926) document the Hebrew-language sources and converso intermediaries central to Christian Kabbalah.

4. **Venetian religious and cultural history**: Martin 1993 (Venice's heretics), Malavasi 2005 (witches and heretics in the Veneto), Pagan 1973–74 (the Accademia Veneziana), and Luzio–Renier 2005 (Isabella d'Este's literary relations) place Zorzi within a specific Venetian and Northern Italian intellectual milieu.

5. **Musical cosmology**: Maillard 1972 (musical aspects of *De Harmonia Mundi*), Magnard 1985 (universal harmony from Giorgio Veneto to Mersenne), Miletto 2005 (Rabbi Yehudah Moscato on the body as musical instrument), and Paczkowski 1990 (Kircher's *Musurgia Universalis*) trace the tradition of musical-cosmological speculation in which Zorzi participates.

### Sample Entries (verbatim)

**MAILLARD 1971**
> J.-F. Maillard, *Le «De Harmonia mundi» de Georges de Venise. Aperçus sur la genèse et la structure de l'oeuvre*, in «Revue de l'histoire des religions» 179 (1971), pp. 181-203.

**MAILLARD 1974**
> J.-F. Maillard, *Sous l'invocation de Dante et de Pic de la Mirandole: les manuscrits inédits de Georges de Venise (Francesco Zorzi)*, in «Bibliothèque d'Humanisme et Renaissance» 36 (1974), pp. 47-61.

**MELCZER 1984**
> W. Melczer, *Ermetismo e cabala cristiana nel pensiero di Francesco Zorzi*, in TAFURI 1984, pp. 148-163.

**MUCCILLO 1996**
> M. Muccillo, *Platonismo, ermetismo e «prisca theologia». Ricerche di storiografia filosofica rinascimentale*, Quaderni di «Rinascimento», 34, Olschki, Firenze 1996.

**PERRONE COMPAGNI 1982**
> V. Perrone Compagni, *Una fonte di Cornelio Agrippa, il «De Harmonia Mundi» di France-* [entry continues on following page]

### Conclusion

This bibliography segment is a primary orientation tool for any scholar working on Zorzi, Christian Kabbalah, Venetian Renaissance thought, or the tradition of musical cosmology from Ficino through Mersenne. The density of Maillard entries in particular signals how much of the pre-Campanini Zorzi scholarship was produced by a single French researcher, a debt the Campanini edition systematically consolidates and supersedes.
